policy_statement: Develop a cross-cutting checklist of sustainability criteria (including circular economy) to be integrated into upstream design of each renovation/construction project.
mechanism_description: The city council commits to developing a sustainability checklist that is integrated into early project design so renovation and construction projects incorporate circular-economy practices that reduce embodied emissions from materials and construction.
primary_causal_pathway: Design & procurement choices → ↓ virgin material production + ↓ construction waste → ↓ embodied GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Early-stage building project design, specifications, and procurement are altered via a set/standardized checklist to favor circular practices (reuse of components, design for disassembly, material efficiency, recycled/low-carbon content, longer service life).  
2) Mediating process: Reduced extraction, processing, and manufacturing of emissions-intensive materials (e.g., cement, steel, aluminum) and reduced waste generation/handling due to higher reuse/recycling and less over-ordering.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower life-cycle (embodied) GHG emissions associated with construction/renovation, leading to net mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing embodied GHG emissions by avoiding production of carbon-intensive virgin building materials through circular design choices.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets embodied emissions by changing design/procurement to reduce virgin material demand and construction waste, yielding direct GHG reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While circularity can improve resource efficiency, the stated and dominant climate-relevant endpoint is reduced embodied carbon (a mitigation outcome), not primarily reduced operational energy use or climate hazard exposure.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a checklist focused mainly on waste diversion without clear GHG relevance, it could drift toward Resource Efficiency; if it emphasized passive survivability/heat resilience, it could drift toward Adaptation. Under this canonical mechanism (“…→ embodied_carbon_reduction”), the main effect remains mitigation.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy implementing a circular-economy design checklist explicitly aimed at embodied carbon reduction operates through reduced life-cycle material-related emissions, so Mitigation is the correct primary category across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further sub-classification (e.g., procurement vs. permitting vs. guidance-only; specific material levers like concrete/steel) requires policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the checklist is purely voluntary or primarily administrative with weak uptake, real-world mitigation impact may be small, but the dominant intended causal pathway is still embodied GHG reduction (Mitigation). If the checklist heavily prioritizes recycling logistics or waste management over embodied-carbon accounting, some implementations may align more with Resource Efficiency, though that would reflect a different canonical mechanism.
key_indicators: “developing a cross-cutting checklist of sustainability criteria”; “including the circular economy dimension”; “integrated into the upstream thinking”; “right from the design stage”; “reversibility, flexibility, pooling, reuse”
co_benefits: Reduced construction and demolition waste; resource efficiency/material cost savings; improved adaptability of buildings; local reuse/recycling market stimulation.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be treated as a procurement requirement or regulatory mandate if the checklist is formally binding as a pass/fail project gateway; text emphasizes development/integration into design thinking rather than an explicit enforceable requirement.
